nodes.title: Ad Hominem
nodes.oneLiner: Attacking the person making the argument instead of the argument itself.

nodes.title: Affect Heuristic
nodes.oneLiner: We make judgements based on our current emotions rather than objective analysis.

nodes.title: Anchoring Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The first number or piece of information we hear disproportionately shapes everything that follows.

nodes.title: Appeal to Emotion
nodes.oneLiner: Substituting feelings for evidence to win an argument.

nodes.title: Appeal to False Authority
nodes.oneLiner: Using a source that sounds authoritative but isn't actually reliable or relevant as the foundation of an argument.

nodes.title: Appeal to Nature
nodes.oneLiner: The assumption that something is good because it's natural, or bad because it's artificial.

nodes.title: Authority Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We give disproportionate weight to the opinions of people we perceive as authorities - even outside their expertise.

nodes.title: Availability Heuristic
nodes.oneLiner: We judge how likely something is based on how easily an example comes to mind.

nodes.title: Aversive Racism
nodes.oneLiner: When people who genuinely believe they are not prejudiced still harbour unconscious biases that shape their behaviour in subtle ways.

nodes.title: Backfire Effect
nodes.oneLiner: Correcting someone's false belief can actually make them believe it more strongly.

nodes.title: Bandwagon Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We're more likely to believe or do something if lots of other people already do.

nodes.title: Ben Franklin Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We grow to like people we've done favours for, not just people who've done favours for us.

nodes.title: Betrayal Aversion
nodes.oneLiner: Harm that comes with a violation of trust hurts far more than the same harm without it.

nodes.title: Burden of Proof
nodes.oneLiner: Demanding that someone disprove your claim, rather than proving it yourself.

nodes.title: Bystander Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any one person is to help.

nodes.title: Circle of Competence
nodes.oneLiner: Knowing the boundaries of what you actually understand - and being honest about where those boundaries lie.

nodes.title: Circular Reasoning
nodes.oneLiner: Using your conclusion as your premise - the argument proves itself by assuming itself.

nodes.title: Cognitive Dissonance
nodes.oneLiner: The uncomfortable tension we feel when holding two contradictory beliefs at the same time.

nodes.title: Compartmentalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Holding contradictory beliefs or values by keeping them in separate mental boxes that never touch.

nodes.title: Compassion Fatigue
nodes.oneLiner: Repeated exposure to suffering gradually reduces your capacity to care.

nodes.title: Concern Trolling
nodes.oneLiner: Pretending to be sympathetic or worried in order to undermine.

nodes.title: Confirmation Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We seek out information that supports what we already believe, and ignore what doesn't.

nodes.title: DARVO
nodes.oneLiner: Deny the behaviour, Attack the person who raised it, Reverse the roles of Victim and Offender.

nodes.title: Denial
nodes.oneLiner: Refusing to accept reality because it's too threatening to process.

nodes.title: Dog Whistling
nodes.oneLiner: Using coded language that sounds innocent to most people but carries a specific prejudiced message to a target audience.

nodes.title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The less you know about something, the more confident you're likely to feel about it.

nodes.title: Emergence
nodes.oneLiner: Complex behaviours arising from simple rules, with no central plan or control.

nodes.title: Euphemism
nodes.oneLiner: Softening an unpleasant reality with gentler, vaguer words.

nodes.title: Expectancy Violation
nodes.oneLiner: When someone behaves differently from what we expected, our emotional reaction is amplified - for better or worse.

nodes.title: False Balance
nodes.oneLiner: Presenting two sides as equally valid when the evidence overwhelmingly favours one.

nodes.title: False Consensus Effect
nodes.oneLiner: We tend to assume that most people think the way we do - and we're usually wrong.

nodes.title: False Dilemma
nodes.oneLiner: When someone presents only two options, even though more exist.

nodes.title: False Equivalence
nodes.oneLiner: Treating two things as equally valid or important when they clearly aren't.

nodes.title: Feedback Loops
nodes.oneLiner: When the output of a system feeds back in as input, either amplifying or dampening the original effect.

nodes.title: Firehose of Falsehood
nodes.oneLiner: Flooding the information space with so many lies, half-truths, and contradictions that people give up trying to figure out what's true.

nodes.title: First Principles Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Breaking a problem down to its most fundamental truths and reasoning up from there, rather than relying on analogy or convention.

nodes.title: Framing Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The way information is presented changes how we respond to it - even when the underlying facts are identical.

nodes.title: Fundamental Attribution Error
nodes.oneLiner: When others mess up, we blame their character. When we mess up, we blame the situation.

nodes.title: Gaslighting
nodes.oneLiner: Making someone doubt their own perception of reality.

nodes.title: Gish Gallop
nodes.oneLiner: Overwhelming someone with a flood of arguments, regardless of their accuracy, so they can't possibly respond to them all.

nodes.title: Halo Effect
nodes.oneLiner: One positive trait colours your entire perception of a person, product, or idea.

nodes.title: Hanlon's Razor
nodes.oneLiner: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by incompetence, ignorance, or accident.

nodes.title: Hasty Generalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Drawing a broad conclusion from too few examples.

nodes.title: Hindsight Bias
nodes.oneLiner: After something happens, we convince ourselves we knew it all along.

nodes.title: Illusory Truth Effect
nodes.oneLiner: The more often we hear something, the more likely we are to believe it - regardless of whether it's true.

nodes.title: In-group/Out-group Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We automatically trust people who seem like us and distrust people who don't.

nodes.title: Independent Evaluation
nodes.oneLiner: Forming your own judgement before hearing what everyone else thinks.

nodes.title: Inversion
nodes.oneLiner: Instead of asking how to succeed, ask how you would guarantee failure - then avoid those things.

nodes.title: Just-World Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: The belief that people generally get what they deserve - that the world is fundamentally fair.

nodes.title: Learned Helplessness
nodes.oneLiner: When repeated failure or lack of control teaches us to stop trying - even when things change.

nodes.title: Loaded Language
nodes.oneLiner: Words chosen for their emotional charge rather than their accuracy.

nodes.title: Loss Aversion
nodes.oneLiner: Losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good.

nodes.title: Manufactured Consent
nodes.oneLiner: When media systems produce public agreement with elite interests - not through censorship, but through structure.

nodes.title: Map is Not the Territory
nodes.oneLiner: Every model of reality is a simplification - useful, but never the full picture.

nodes.title: Microaggressions
nodes.oneLiner: Small, everyday slights and indignities - often unintentional - that communicate hostility or prejudice toward members of marginalised groups.

nodes.title: Moral Hypocrisy Judgement
nodes.oneLiner: We punish the contradiction between someone's stated values and their behaviour more harshly than we punish the behaviour alone.

nodes.title: Moral Licensing
nodes.oneLiner: Doing something good gives you unconscious permission to do something bad.

nodes.title: Moral Panic
nodes.oneLiner: Intense public fear about a perceived threat, amplified by media, disproportionate to the actual danger.

nodes.title: Motivated Reasoning
nodes.oneLiner: Using our intelligence not to find truth but to defend conclusions we've already reached.

nodes.title: Motte-and-Bailey
nodes.oneLiner: Holding a controversial position but retreating to a much more defensible one when challenged - then switching back once the pressure's off.

nodes.title: Moving the Goalposts
nodes.oneLiner: Changing the criteria for proof after the original criteria have been met.

nodes.title: Naive Realism
nodes.oneLiner: The belief that you see the world objectively - and that anyone who disagrees must be uninformed, irrational, or biased.

nodes.title: Negativity Bias
nodes.oneLiner: Bad experiences affect us more powerfully than equally good ones.

nodes.title: Normalcy Bias
nodes.oneLiner: The assumption that because things have always been a certain way, they will continue to be.

nodes.title: Occam's Razor
nodes.oneLiner: The simplest explanation that fits the facts is usually the right one.

nodes.title: Opportunity Cost
nodes.oneLiner: Every choice has a hidden price: the thing you didn't choose.

nodes.title: Overton Window
nodes.oneLiner: The range of ideas the public considers acceptable at any given time - and how that range can be deliberately shifted.

nodes.title: Paradox of Tolerance
nodes.oneLiner: A tolerant society that tolerates intolerance will eventually be destroyed by it.

nodes.title: Pluralistic Ignorance
nodes.oneLiner: When most people in a group privately disagree with a norm but go along with it because they assume everyone else agrees.

nodes.title: Post Hoc
nodes.oneLiner: Assuming that because one thing followed another, the first thing caused the second.

nodes.title: Probabilistic Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Thinking in likelihoods rather than certainties - because almost nothing is truly yes or no.

nodes.title: Psychological Projection
nodes.oneLiner: Attributing your own feelings, motives, or behaviours to someone else.

nodes.title: Rationalisation
nodes.oneLiner: Constructing a logical-sounding justification for a decision you actually made for emotional or self-serving reasons.

nodes.title: Red Herring
nodes.oneLiner: Introducing something irrelevant to divert attention from the actual issue.

nodes.title: Relative Deprivation
nodes.oneLiner: Feeling worse off based on who you compare yourself to, not on what you actually have.

nodes.title: Scapegoating
nodes.oneLiner: Blaming a person or group for problems they didn't cause.

nodes.title: Sealioning
nodes.oneLiner: Endlessly demanding evidence or explanations in bad faith, disguised as polite curiosity.

nodes.title: Second-Order Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: Asking 'and then what?' - looking beyond the immediate consequences to what happens next.

nodes.title: Slippery Slope
nodes.oneLiner: Arguing that one step will inevitably lead to an extreme outcome, without justifying the chain of events in between.

nodes.title: Social Proof
nodes.oneLiner: Looking to other people's behaviour to decide what's correct.

nodes.title: Source Laundering
nodes.oneLiner: Passing unreliable information through credible-looking channels until it appears legitimate.

nodes.title: State of Nature Assumption
nodes.oneLiner: The unconscious belief about whether people are fundamentally selfish or fundamentally cooperative - and how that shapes everything else you think.

nodes.title: Straw Man
nodes.oneLiner: Misrepresenting someone's argument to make it easier to attack.

nodes.title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.oneLiner: Continuing to invest in something because of what you've already put in, not because of what you'll get out.

nodes.title: Survivorship Bias
nodes.oneLiner: We only see the winners, so we misjudge what it takes to succeed.

nodes.title: Symbolic Racism
nodes.oneLiner: When prejudice is expressed not through overt hostility but through opposition to policies and positions associated with marginalised groups, framed in the language of fairness and principle.

nodes.title: Thought-Terminating Cliche
nodes.oneLiner: A phrase that sounds wise but actually shuts down critical thinking.

nodes.title: Tone Policing
nodes.oneLiner: Dismissing an argument by criticising how it's being made rather than what's being said.

nodes.title: Tragedy of the Commons
nodes.oneLiner: When individuals acting in their own rational interest collectively destroy a shared resource.

nodes.title: Unintended Consequences
nodes.oneLiner: Actions in complex systems produce outcomes nobody planned for.

nodes.title: Victim-Perpetrator Cycle
nodes.oneLiner: Groups that have suffered persecution can, once they gain power, go on to persecute others - often using their past suffering as justification.

nodes.title: Weasel Words
nodes.oneLiner: Language that sounds specific but commits to nothing.

nodes.title: Whataboutism
nodes.oneLiner: Deflecting criticism by pointing to someone else's wrongdoing instead of addressing the original point.

nodes.title: Woozle Effect
nodes.oneLiner: When a weak or unsupported claim gets cited and re-cited until it starts looking like established fact.

nodes.title: Zero-Sum Thinking
nodes.oneLiner: The assumption that any situation is a competition where one person's gain must be another's loss.

notes.text: An ad hominem is a specific type of red herring - it diverts attention from the argument to the arguer.
Both avoid engaging with the actual argument - straw man distorts it, ad hominem ignores it entirely.
The mirror image of ad hominem - instead of dismissing an argument because of who made it, you accept it because of who made it.
Persistent ad hominem attacks can be used as a form of sealioning - wearing someone down by making the conversation about them rather than the issue.
The affect heuristic and availability heuristic often fire together - vivid memories trigger emotional responses that shape judgement
The availability heuristic and the affect heuristic often work together - vivid memories trigger emotional responses that shape judgement
Framing works partly by triggering different emotional responses to the same information
Anchoring is a specific form of framing - the first piece of information frames everything after it
Anchoring is a specific form of framing - the first number frames everything after it
Extreme anchors can shift what feels like a reasonable middle ground
Extreme positions anchor the debate, making the merely unreasonable feel like a compromise
The cognitive bias that makes appeal to emotion work - we judge things by how they make us feel.
How the same facts can trigger completely different emotional responses depending on how they're presented.
We're more susceptible to emotional appeals that confirm what we already believe.
Once an emotional appeal has landed, motivated reasoning kicks in to justify the conclusion we've already reached.
Often paired with emotional appeals - distort someone's position, then react emotionally to the distortion.
The cognitive bias that makes us susceptible to this fallacy - we're wired to defer to perceived authority figures
What happens next - the false authority's claim gets cited and re-cited until it looks like established fact
Often the starting point - a dubious source gets treated as authoritative, and the Woozle Effect does the rest
The deliberate version - routing unreliable claims through credible-looking channels
Source laundering manufactures the false authority that this fallacy then exploits
We're more likely to accept a dubious authority if they're telling us what we already believe
'Natural' triggers a positive emotional response that bypasses rational evaluation
The Dunning-Kruger effect explains why we should be cautious about self-appointed authorities
Authority bias is one of the strongest forces that independent evaluation protects against
Negative events are more vivid and memorable, making them more 'available'
Negative events are more vivid and memorable, making them more 'available' to recall
Dog whistles work partly because they activate the unconscious biases that aversive racism describes
Dog whistling works partly because it activates prejudices people don't consciously acknowledge
Where aversive racism is unconscious and personal, symbolic racism is its political expression
Aversive racism operates at the personal and unconscious level; symbolic racism is how it shows up in political attitudes
Microaggressions are often the visible surface of aversive racism - the moments where unconscious bias leaks into behaviour
Microaggressions are often the visible surface of the unconscious biases that aversive racism describes
The deeper tribal wiring that aversive racism builds on
The discomfort when someone who believes they are fair is confronted with evidence of their own bias
The backfire effect is cognitive dissonance taken to its extreme - the brain protects the belief at any cost
What happens when cognitive dissonance gets so intense that correction strengthens the original belief
What happens when someone tries to correct a belief held through confirmation bias
The backfire effect is what happens when motivated reasoning wins the fight against evidence
The bandwagon effect and pluralistic ignorance feed each other - we join the crowd, assuming the crowd knows something we don't
Pluralistic ignorance creates false bandwagons - everyone joins because they think everyone else is already on board
The direct antidote to the bandwagon effect
Independent evaluation is the antidote to the bandwagon effect
The Ben Franklin Effect is cognitive dissonance in action - we helped them, so we must like them
Part of the pain of betrayal is confronting our own misjudgement
Betrayal aversion is what happens when a positive expectation is violated by someone we trusted
Betrayal aversion is a specific case of expectancy violation - where the violated expectation was trust
We punish the contradiction between stated values and behaviour, not just the behaviour itself
Betrayal aversion is about broken trust. Moral hypocrisy judgement is about broken consistency between values and actions.
The 'every accusation is a confession' pattern - sometimes the loudest virtue-signallers are the worst offenders
Both make positions unfalsifiable - circular reasoning by looping back on itself, burden-shifting by demanding impossible disproof.
Sometimes the burden is shifted by invoking authority: 'Experts say X, prove them wrong' - even when the experts haven't provided evidence either.
Post hoc claims often exploit burden of proof - 'I took the remedy and got better, prove that it didn't help' puts the burden on the sceptic.
Weasel words help shift the burden by making claims vague enough that they're hard to disprove: 'Some people say...' - prove that no one says it.
Pluralistic ignorance feeds the bystander effect - when nobody reacts, each person interprets the group's inaction as a signal that action isn't needed.
Both involve the gap between individual and collective responsibility - each person assumes someone else will carry the burden.
Normalcy bias reinforces bystander inaction - 'If it were really serious, someone would have done something by now.'
The bystander effect is the bandwagon effect in reverse - inaction is contagious in the same way action can be.
Dunning-Kruger explains why people misjudge their circle of competence - the less you know, the less you know about what you don't know.
Naive realism makes your circle of competence feel bigger than it is - your understanding feels like objective truth rather than a limited perspective.
First principles thinking helps you stay within your circle - it forces you to identify what you actually know versus what you're assuming.
Your circle of competence is a map - useful within its boundaries, dangerously misleading beyond them.
Circular reasoning is the logical structure; confirmation bias is the psychological mechanism that keeps it feeling valid.
Circular arguments often end in thought-terminating cliches: 'It's true because it's true. End of.'
Circular reasoning sometimes hides behind authority: 'The policy is correct because the experts designed it, and we know they're experts because they designed correct policies.'
Motivated reasoning naturally produces circular logic - we start with the conclusion we want and construct reasoning that leads back to it.
The tool our brains use to resolve cognitive dissonance without actually changing our minds
Motivated reasoning is the brain's primary tool for resolving cognitive dissonance without changing beliefs
We avoid dissonance by seeking information that confirms what we already believe
Projection is a way of externalising the dissonance between who we think we are and how we actually behave
Compartmentalisation is an alternative to resolving cognitive dissonance - instead of reconciling contradictions, you prevent them from meeting.
Rationalisation explains away contradictions; compartmentalisation prevents you from noticing them in the first place.
Denial refuses to see a threatening fact. Compartmentalisation sees it but quarantines it from the beliefs it would threaten.
Moral licensing often relies on compartmentalisation - keeping the 'good me' and the 'indulgent me' in separate compartments.
Compassion fatigue and learned helplessness feed each other - when caring never seems to change anything, you stop caring and stop trying.
Constant exposure to suffering via media makes it feel both overwhelming and, paradoxically, normal - the availability heuristic in two directions.
Compassion fatigue can look like normalcy bias - not because you think everything is fine, but because your emotional response to 'not fine' has been exhausted.
Negativity bias ensures that bad news dominates attention - and the relentless weight of it is exactly what produces compassion fatigue.
Both disguise hostility as civility - sealioning as 'just asking questions', concern trolling as 'just looking out for you'.
Concern trolling about optics ('I worry this will put people off') is tone policing dressed up as strategy advice.
The concern troll's motte is 'I support your goals' - a defensible position they retreat to when challenged on their actual intent.
The expressed 'concern' is itself a red herring - it shifts discussion from the issue to the tactical question of presentation.
The engine behind confirmation bias - we use our intelligence to defend conclusions we've already reached
Confirmation bias is the passive version - motivated reasoning is the active version
Repeated exposure to confirming information makes it feel even more true
Confirmation bias primes us to accept repeated claims that align with existing beliefs
DARVO often includes gaslighting - the denial phase makes the accuser question whether the event happened at all.
The reversal phase of DARVO is projection in action - the offender projects victim status onto themselves.
The attack phase often uses whataboutism - 'What about all the times YOU did something wrong?'
The attack phase of DARVO is a form of ad hominem - discrediting the accuser rather than addressing the accusation.
Normalcy bias and denial work together - normalcy bias says 'it's fine', denial says 'I refuse to consider that it might not be'.
Rationalisation is often the next step after denial begins to crack - when you can no longer deny the facts, you start explaining them away.
Denial is the most blunt instrument for managing cognitive dissonance - rather than reconcile contradictory information, you simply reject one side of it.
The backfire effect can be denial in action - when confronted with evidence against a core belief, denial hardens the belief rather than softening it.
Denial is the extreme end of motivated reasoning - not just seeking confirming evidence, but refusing to acknowledge disconfirming evidence altogether.
The political expression of prejudice that dog whistling is designed to activate
Dog whistling is the rhetorical delivery system for symbolic racism - the coded language that activates it
Dog whistling is a specific form of framing - choosing words that trigger particular associations
When challenged, dog whistlers retreat to the innocent literal meaning - a classic motte-and-bailey move
Where dog whistling is public and strategic, microaggressions are the personal and often unconscious equivalent
Where dog whistling is public and strategic, microaggressions are personal and usually unconscious - but both rely on coded communication
Emergent behaviours often arise from feedback loops operating between many individual agents.
Emergence is, in a sense, the systemic cousin of unintended consequences - complex outcomes nobody planned.
Social norms can emerge from pluralistic ignorance - everyone follows a rule that nobody privately endorses.
Trends and movements can emerge from individual bandwagon decisions cascading through a population.
Two sides of the same coin - loaded language amplifies emotion, euphemism dampens it.
Euphemism is a specific framing technique - it frames uncomfortable truths as manageable ones.
Both use indirect language, but dog whistles encode a hidden meaning while euphemisms obscure an obvious one.
Euphemism feeds normalcy bias by making alarming situations sound routine.
Normalcy bias is what happens when we refuse to update our expectations even when the evidence demands it
False equivalence is the logical fallacy; false balance is the media practice that institutionalises it.
False balance is a specific kind of framing - it frames a near-consensus as a genuine debate.
False balance often elevates unqualified voices to appear as credible as genuine experts.
Repeated false balance makes fringe positions feel more mainstream than they are.
Confirmation bias filters what we notice; the false consensus effect filters what we assume about everyone else.
Two sides of the same coin - false consensus makes us think everyone agrees with us, pluralistic ignorance makes us think everyone agrees with each other.
The false consensus effect can create phantom bandwagons - we assume the crowd already agrees, so we never check.
We estimate what 'most people' think based on the examples most available to us - which are usually people like us.
We spend more time with people who share our views, which inflates our sense of how common those views are.
Naive realism says 'I see the world as it is' - the false consensus effect adds 'and so does everyone else.'
Often used together - a false dilemma paired with a distorted version of the other option
Often used together - straw man the opposing view, then present a false choice
How the options are presented shapes which one seems reasonable
False equivalence often creates a false dilemma - two positions framed as the only options, treated as equally valid
Feedback loops are what you find when you follow second-order thinking far enough - effects producing effects producing effects.
The illusory truth effect is a cognitive feedback loop - repetition breeds belief, which breeds more repetition.
Social popularity is a classic reinforcing loop - the more people join, the more others want to join.
Normalcy bias can mask a dangerous reinforcing loop - each small change feels normal until the cumulative effect is overwhelming.
The firehose ensures claims are repeated often enough to trigger the illusory truth effect
The firehose strategy deliberately exploits the illusory truth effect through sheer volume
The Gish gallop is the debate-scale version; the firehose is the society-scale version
The industrialised, state-level version of the same tactic
The firehose's ultimate goal - a population too exhausted to seek truth
The firehose strategy's ultimate goal is learned helplessness - people giving up on determining what's true
Both cut through complexity - Occam's Razor by favouring simplicity, first principles by stripping back to fundamentals.
Independent evaluation is first principles thinking applied to claims - assessing evidence directly rather than deferring to consensus.
First principles gets you to the foundation; second-order thinking builds forward from there to anticipate consequences.
First principles thinking is the antidote to sunk cost - it asks 'what should we do from here?' not 'what have we already done?'
Negative frames are more powerful because of our built-in negativity bias
Framing something as a loss rather than a gain exploits negativity bias
We're more likely to make generous attributions for people in our group and harsh ones for outsiders.
The just-world fallacy is attribution error at scale - if bad things happen to people, they must deserve it.
Attributing our own failures to circumstances protects us from the dissonance of admitting a character flaw.
Naive realism makes attribution error feel justified - if I see reality clearly and you disagree, there must be something wrong with you.
Gaslighters often project - accusing the target of the very behaviours they themselves are engaged in.
Gaslighting deliberately creates cognitive dissonance - a gap between what you experienced and what you're being told happened.
Targets of gaslighting may engage in motivated reasoning to preserve the relationship - finding reasons to believe the gaslighter rather than their own memory.
Gaslighters frequently use whataboutism to deflect - turning any complaint back on the person raising it.
A different bad-faith debate tactic - overwhelming through endless questions rather than endless claims
Sealioning overwhelms through questions; the Gish gallop overwhelms through claims. Both exploit the asymmetry between asking and answering.
The halo effect explains part of why authority bias works - we assume competence in one domain extends to others.
Popularity creates its own halo - we assume popular things must be good because so many people like them.
Once the halo is in place, confirmation bias keeps it there - we notice evidence that supports our positive impression and ignore what contradicts it.
The halo effect is a specific instance of the affect heuristic - our feelings about one trait spill over into our judgement of everything else.
Hanlon's Razor is the direct antidote to attribution error - it pushes you to consider situational explanations before jumping to character judgements.
Both razors cut in the same direction - toward simpler explanations. Occam's favours fewer assumptions; Hanlon's favours less malice.
Naive realism makes us assume that if someone's actions are harmful, they must have intended the harm - because we can't imagine seeing the situation differently.
Many outcomes that look like deliberate plans are actually emergent - the product of many small, uncoordinated mistakes rather than one big conspiracy.
Vivid examples feel representative even when they aren't - the availability heuristic is what makes hasty generalisations feel so convincing.
Once a generalisation is formed, confirmation bias preserves it - we notice every confirming example and ignore every exception.
We generalise far more about out-groups than in-groups. One member of 'them' represents them all; one member of 'us' is just an individual.
Survivorship bias is a specific form of hasty generalisation - drawing conclusions from a non-representative sample of survivors.
Hindsight bias is partly driven by confirmation bias - after the outcome, we selectively remember the evidence that pointed toward it.
Once something has happened, the outcome is vivid and available - making it feel like it was always the most likely result.
Both distort our view of the past - hindsight bias makes outcomes feel inevitable, survivorship bias makes success look easier than it was.
Hindsight bias inflates our sense of our own judgement, feeding the overconfidence that Dunning-Kruger describes.
In-group bias filters information the same way confirmation bias does - through the lens of belonging
Others' opinions act as anchors that pull your judgement towards them
Both are tools for thinking beyond the obvious - second-order thinking asks 'then what?', inversion asks 'what if the opposite?'
Inversion and first principles thinking work beautifully together - strip back to fundamentals, then look at the problem from both directions.
Inversion helps anticipate unintended consequences by deliberately imagining what could go wrong.
Inversion can break the sunk cost spell: instead of asking 'should I continue?', ask 'if I weren't already in this, would I start it today?'
Survivorship bias feeds the just-world fallacy - we see the winners and assume they deserved to win
Survivorship bias feeds the belief that success is always deserved
The just-world fallacy is a way of avoiding the dissonance that comes from living in an unfair world
Framing chooses what to emphasise; loaded language chooses how to describe it.
Dog whistles are loaded language with a hidden audience - words that carry one meaning publicly and another to those in the know.
Loaded language is one of the primary delivery mechanisms for emotional appeals.
Some loaded phrases work as both - emotionally charged and conversation-ending.
Sunk cost fallacy is loss aversion in action - we can't let go of past investments because writing them off feels like a loss.
Loss aversion is why framing matters so much - the same choice described as a loss or a gain produces completely different decisions.
Loss aversion partly explains normalcy bias - change feels like loss, so we cling to the current state even when it's deteriorating.
Loss aversion and negativity bias are close cousins - both reflect the brain's tendency to weight negative experiences more heavily than positive ones.
Manufactured consent operates largely through framing - not lying about events, but controlling how they're presented and which ones matter.
Manufactured consent helps define the Overton Window - the range of ideas that feel acceptable is partly shaped by what media treats as serious.
Two different models of propaganda - manufactured consent works through omission and framing, the firehose works through overwhelming volume.
False balance is one mechanism through which consent is manufactured - by framing elite-friendly positions as the 'balanced' centre.
Repeated framing across multiple outlets triggers the illusory truth effect - the consensus feels earned when it's actually structural.
First principles thinking is a way of going back to the territory when the existing maps are misleading.
Naive realism is the belief that your map IS the territory - that your perception of reality is reality itself.
Every frame is a map - it highlights some features of reality and hides others.
Occam's Razor reminds us that simpler maps are often more useful - but simplicity always means leaving things out.
The tribal instinct that makes us unconsciously treat 'outsiders' differently in small, everyday ways
People on the receiving end sometimes normalise microaggressions because they are so constant - and people committing them normalise their own behaviour for the same reason
Bystanders often stay silent during microaggressions because they assume nobody else noticed or minded
Projection and moral hypocrisy often travel together - the loudest moralisers are sometimes projecting their own failings
The hypocrite is experiencing cognitive dissonance between their stated values and their behaviour. We're experiencing it too, when we discover the gap.
Moral licensing is a way of pre-emptively managing dissonance - the good act provides a justification that prevents the bad act from feeling contradictory.
Moral licensing is motivated reasoning applied to self-perception - we construct a narrative in which our indulgence is deserved.
The 'I've earned this' reasoning is a form of rationalisation - constructing a logical-sounding case for a decision made on other grounds.
The 'I've earned this' reasoning that powers moral licensing is a specific form of rationalisation.
We notice moral licensing in others ('hypocrite!') more easily than in ourselves - attribution error makes our own licensing feel earned.
Moral panics exploit the availability heuristic - intense media coverage makes the threat feel more common and immediate than it is.
Every moral panic needs a villain. Scapegoating provides one - a group that can be blamed for the perceived threat.
Moral panics are sustained by emotional appeal - fear, outrage, and disgust rather than evidence and proportion.
Moral panics can serve the same function as manufactured consent - aligning public attention and opinion with elite interests.
The language of moral panics is always loaded - 'epidemic', 'invasion', 'plague', 'crisis' - words chosen to escalate rather than inform.
The straw man distorts the opponent's position. The motte-and-bailey distorts your own.
The motte-and-bailey is a kind of reverse straw man - distorting your own position rather than your opponent's
Sealioning often involves moving the goalposts - each answered question produces another question, with no endpoint in sight.
Both create unfalsifiable positions - circular reasoning defines its way out of challenge, goalpost-moving raises the bar each time it's met.
Related evasion tactics - motte-and-bailey retreats to a safer claim when challenged, goalpost-moving changes what counts as meeting the challenge.
Both exhaust the other person - Gish Gallop through volume, goalpost-moving through an endless series of shifting demands.
Confirmation bias keeps naive realism alive - we constantly encounter 'evidence' that our view is correct because we're selectively noticing it.
Naive realism fuels in-group/out-group bias: our group sees things clearly, their group is deluded.
Both involve a failure to recognise the limits of your own perspective - Dunning-Kruger about competence, naive realism about perception.
Motivated reasoning operates invisibly, which is exactly why naive realism persists - we can't see the filters we're looking through.
Normalcy bias makes us slow to notice when the Overton Window is shifting beneath our feet
We assume the current window is stable, making us slow to recognise when it's shifting
Normalcy bias stops us from thinking through second and third-order effects of change
We often prefer complicated explanations because they fit a narrative we already believe
Sunk cost fallacy ignores opportunity cost entirely - it focuses on what you've already spent rather than what you could spend your resources on next.
Loss aversion makes opportunity costs invisible - the potential loss of what you have looms larger than the potential gain of what you could have.
Second-order thinking naturally surfaces opportunity costs - when you ask 'and then what?', you start seeing the paths not taken.
Inversion helps reveal opportunity costs: instead of 'should I do X?', ask 'what am I not doing because I'm doing X?'
The paradox of tolerance is about what happens at the edges of the Overton Window - when intolerant ideas are normalised into the range of acceptable debate.
False balance can enable the paradox - giving extremist positions equal airtime in the name of fairness helps normalise them.
The paradox is often misused as a false dilemma: 'either we tolerate everything or we censor everything.' Popper's point was more nuanced than that.
Intolerant movements often use motte-and-bailey tactics - defending extreme positions by retreating to reasonable-sounding versions when challenged.
Confirmation bias locks in post hoc reasoning - once we believe X caused Y, we selectively notice every time the pattern seems to repeat.
If a false causal claim is repeated often enough, it starts to feel established - the mechanism behind many persistent myths.
Vivid or recent coincidences feel more causally connected because they're more mentally available.
Post hoc reasoning often gets laundered through authority - 'studies show that X leads to Y' when the studies show only correlation.
The availability heuristic is the enemy of probabilistic thinking - it substitutes 'easy to imagine' for 'likely to happen'.
Confirmation bias distorts our probability estimates by overweighting evidence that supports our existing beliefs.
First principles gives you the foundation; probabilistic thinking helps you reason about uncertain outcomes built on that foundation.
Occam's Razor is a probabilistic heuristic - simpler explanations aren't guaranteed to be correct, but they're more likely to be.
False dilemmas force binary thinking. Probabilistic thinking resists this by recognising that most situations involve a spectrum of possible outcomes.
Motivated reasoning is the search for supporting evidence; rationalisation is the story you tell about what you found.
Rationalisation is one of the primary tools for resolving cognitive dissonance - it bridges the gap between what you did and what you believe.
Sunk cost decisions are almost always accompanied by rationalisation: 'I've come this far, it would be wasteful to stop now.'
Confirmation bias provides the raw material; rationalisation weaves it into a convincing narrative.
A straw man distorts the argument; a red herring abandons it entirely for a different one.
Emotional appeals are often used as red herrings - they shift attention from the logical question to an emotional one.
Whataboutism is a specific type of red herring - diverting from the issue by pointing to someone else's behaviour.
The Gish Gallop floods a debate with so many points that the original issue gets buried - red herrings at industrial scale.
Zero-sum thinking is the cognitive frame that makes relative deprivation feel rational - if someone else is gaining, I must be losing.
Relative deprivation is what zero-sum thinking feels like from the inside - the sense that your position is slipping even when your absolute situation hasn't changed.
Losing relative position activates the same psychology as losing something tangible - it hurts more than an equivalent gain in absolute terms would help.
Relative deprivation hits hardest when the comparison is with an out-group - 'they' are getting what 'we' deserve.
Relative deprivation depends entirely on which comparison is made salient - change the reference point and the same situation feels like comfort or injustice.
Social media and news make upward comparisons constantly available, amplifying relative deprivation far beyond what we'd experience in daily life.
Media and political systems can manufacture relative deprivation by choosing which comparisons to put in front of people.
Scapegoating exploits the in-group/out-group divide - it only works when the target is already seen as 'other'.
Visible minorities make easy scapegoats because they're cognitively available - easy to picture and easy to blame.
Scapegoating often relies on a false dilemma: either we deal with this group, or the problem gets worse.
Scapegoating is powered by emotional appeal - fear, anger, and resentment rather than evidence.
Scapegoating reframes a complex systemic problem as a simple story with a clear villain.
Slippery slope arguments work by triggering fear of an extreme outcome rather than by providing evidence for the chain of events.
Both flatten complexity - false dilemmas reduce options to two, slippery slopes reduce a nuanced process to a single inevitable trajectory.
Slippery slope reframes a modest proposal as the first step toward catastrophe.
Some slippery slopes are real - when genuine reinforcing feedback loops exist, one step can accelerate the next. The fallacy is assuming this without evidence.
The bandwagon effect is social proof applied to beliefs and trends - joining in because others have.
Social proof's dark twin - when everyone follows the group signal, but the signal itself is wrong because nobody actually believes it.
Social proof and repetition reinforce each other - the more people repeat something, the truer it feels.
Authority is a specific form of social proof - we look to experts and leaders as signals for what to think.
The halo effect makes social proof from attractive or high-status sources disproportionately persuasive.
The organic cousin - where the Woozle Effect happens through careless repetition, source laundering achieves the same result by design
The deliberate cousin - where the Woozle Effect happens organically, source laundering engineers the same outcome on purpose
Often used together - flood the zone with laundered claims and legitimate fact-checking can't keep up
Another volume-based tactic - laundered sources provide the ammunition for rapid-fire claims
The cognitive bias being exploited - we instinctively trust information that comes from credible-looking channels
Your state of nature assumption acts as an invisible frame - it determines which evidence looks relevant and which arguments feel persuasive
Once you've absorbed a view of human nature, you'll unconsciously seek out evidence that confirms it and dismiss evidence that doesn't
Hobbesians often apply their cynicism selectively - 'my group' is trustworthy, it's 'other people' who can't be trusted
The belief that people get what they deserve often rests on a Hobbesian assumption - if someone is suffering, they must have made bad choices
What counts as a 'reasonable' policy position shifts depending on which view of human nature dominates the culture
Sunk costs fuel motivated reasoning - we look for evidence that our investment was worthwhile because admitting otherwise feels like loss.
Walking away from a sunk cost means admitting the original decision was wrong, which triggers dissonance.
Once invested, we selectively notice evidence that the investment is paying off and ignore signs it isn't.
Sunk cost decisions are heavily influenced by whether we frame the situation as 'protecting an investment' or 'throwing good money after bad'.
We overestimate the likelihood of success because success stories are more visible than failures
The belief that people get what they deserve underpins much of symbolic racism's logic - if the system is fair, inequality must reflect individual failure
Symbolic racism depends on framing - presenting racial opposition as principled concern
People engaging in symbolic racism are often motivated reasoners - reaching a prejudiced conclusion first, then finding principled justifications
Both shape how we process information - framing by choosing what to emphasise, cliches by shutting down further inquiry.
Thought-terminating cliches often work because they feel emotionally satisfying rather than logically sound.
'It's just natural' is itself a thought-terminating cliche.
We're more likely to accept a thought-terminating cliche when it confirms what we already believe.
Tone policing is a form of ad hominem - it redirects attention from the argument to the person making it.
The speaker's tone becomes a red herring - a diversion from the substance of what they're actually saying.
Sealioning demands performative politeness; tone policing punishes the absence of it. Both control the terms of engagement.
Repeated tone policing can function as gaslighting - making someone doubt whether their emotional responses are valid.
The tragedy unfolds because people think in first-order terms (my benefit now) rather than second-order terms (everyone's loss later).
Commons collapse often involves a reinforcing loop - as the resource degrades, competition for what's left intensifies, accelerating the degradation.
Each individual act of overuse feels normal and harmless - it's only the cumulative effect that's catastrophic.
Second-order thinking is the tool for anticipating unintended consequences - asking 'and then what?'
Many unintended consequences emerge from feedback loops the original planners didn't foresee.
Normalcy bias makes us assume the system will absorb our intervention without changing - which is exactly when unintended consequences strike.
The less you understand a complex system, the more confident you are that your intervention will work as planned.
Historical suffering can function as a moral credit, licensing behaviour that would otherwise be condemned
The persecuted group's hardened identity boundaries can flip from defensive to aggressive
Members may struggle to reconcile their group's victim identity with evidence of perpetration
Past trauma is weaponised rhetorically to shut down scrutiny
New targets are identified to channel collective fear and aggression
Weasel words often invoke unnamed authorities - 'experts say' without identifying which experts.
Both manipulate through word choice, but loaded language amplifies emotion while weasel words create the illusion of substance.
Weasel words frame uncertain claims as established facts by hiding the uncertainty in the phrasing.
Weasel words can be a lightweight form of source laundering - attributing claims to anonymous sources lends them unearned credibility.
Whataboutism often implies a false equivalence between the original criticism and the deflection
The cognitive mechanism underneath - repeated exposure to a claim makes it feel true regardless of its accuracy
We're less likely to check the original source when the claim confirms what we already believe
Loss aversion fuels zero-sum thinking - the fear of losing your share feels more urgent than the possibility that everyone's share could grow.
Zero-sum thinking is most powerful when combined with group identity - we're more willing to accept a bad deal if the 'other group' gets an even worse one.
Whether a situation feels zero-sum often depends on how it's framed - the same policy can sound like shared prosperity or a threat to your slice.
Scapegoating relies on zero-sum logic - the implicit argument is always that 'they' are taking what's 'ours'.
Zero-sum thinking is often a false dilemma in disguise - presenting only two options (I win or you win) when cooperation could benefit both.

collections.title: Why We Judge Fallen Heroes More Harshly Than Known Villains
How Bad Information Becomes Common Knowledge
Why We Think Everyone Agrees With Us
Why We'd Rather Have Less Than Let Others Have More
